chunks.text: Nilah Ioannidis is an Adjunct Assistant Professor with a joint appointment in EECS and the Center for Computational Biology (CCB). Her group develops computational methods to analyze and interpret personal genomes, using machine learning and deep learning to predict the clinical impact of genome variation and model variation in gene expression and other molecular phenotypes.

Previously, Dr. Ioannidis was a postdoctoral scholar at Stanford University's Department of Biomedical Data Science, where she developed machine learning tools like the REVEL ensemble predictor for missense variants. During her PhD in Biophysics at Harvard, she worked at MIT's Department of Biological Engineering, creating methods to analyze intracellular particle dynamics using hidden Markov modeling and Bayesian inference.

She also served as Research Director at the Jain Foundation, focusing on the rare genetic disease dysferlinopathy. Recent awards include the Chan Zuckerberg Biohub Investigator Award (2022-2027), Computational Transformation Fellow at UC Noyce Initiative (2022-2023), Okawa Foundation Research Grant (2021), and NIH K99/R00 Pathway to Independence Award (2017-2022).

Her education includes a Ph.D. in Biophysics from Harvard University in 2013. Research areas are Biosystems & Computational Biology (BIO) and Artificial Intelligence (AI), with affiliations to the Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research Lab (BAIR) and Center for Computational Biology (CCB).
